name: Straddle Jump over Vault
description: An early vault skill where your daughter approaches the table, places her hands, and straddles over (legs apart to the sides) before landing. It teaches her to trust the springboard and develop the arm strength and coordination needed for real vaults. Common in recreational gymnastics and early competitive levels.
whatToLookFor: Arms pushing off the table with good strength
Legs going wide out to the sides in a straddle — not in front
Clear height off the table before straddling
Landing with both feet at the same time
commonMistakes: Not pushing off the table hard enough — the push is what gives her height
A weak straddle where the legs barely spread
Landing one foot at a time (should be two feet simultaneously)
Looking down at the ground instead of ahead on landing

quickTipsChecklist.description: Arms push hard to create height
Legs spread clearly to each side
Both feet hit the mat at the same time

name: Handspring with Half Twist
description: A step up from the basic handspring vault, this version adds a half turn in the air so your daughter lands facing the vault table instead of away from it. It requires more coordination and body awareness than the basic handspring but is achievable for recreational and early competitive gymnasts.
whatToLookFor: The same strong handspring onto and off the table as the basic version
A half twist that happens at the peak of her height — not too early, not too late
Landing facing the vault (not sideways or turned too far)
Arms coming down to help control the landing
commonMistakes: Starting the twist before leaving the table — it should happen in the air
An incomplete half twist, landing at 45 degrees instead of fully facing the vault
Losing arm control during the twist, which wobbles the body
Landing too close to the vault because she did not push hard enough off the table

quickTipsChecklist.description: Leaves table fully before beginning the twist
Fully faces the vault on landing
Arms stay close to body during twist for control

name: Yurchenko Pike
description: Instead of staying straight (layout) in the air, this vault features the Yurchenko entry followed by a tight pike (sharp 90-degree bend at the hips with straight legs). The pike position makes the rotation faster but requires tremendous hamstring flexibility and body control. It is more advanced than a basic Yurchenko.
whatToLookFor: The Yurchenko round-off entry looking powerful and timed correctly
A sharp fold at the hips into the pike — legs should be nearly touching her chest
Straight knees throughout the pike position (bent knees reduce the score)
Opening out of the pike in time to land on her feet
Feet together on landing
commonMistakes: A 'soft' pike where the legs are angled instead of sharply folded
Bending the knees inside the pike position
Not opening from the pike in time, causing an awkward landing
Piking too late — should fold immediately after leaving the table

quickTipsChecklist.description: Hips fold to 90 degrees or less
Legs are completely straight throughout
Body opens before landing is reached

name: Glide Kip
description: The kip is the most fundamental bars skill in gymnastics — it's the entry point to everything else on uneven bars. Your daughter hangs from the bar, swings forward (glide), then whips her feet up to the bar and pushes down to rise to a front support position (sitting on top of the bar with straight arms). Every bars routine starts with a kip or ends with one.
whatToLookFor: A big, long glide forward — the longer the glide, the more power for the kip
Toes reaching up to touch the bar as her body swings back
Her hips shooting upward in one quick, powerful motion
Ending up with straight arms and her hips resting on the bar
No piking too early — the timing of the hip push is everything
commonMistakes: A short glide that doesn't generate enough momentum
Pulling with the arms instead of using the hip push — the kip is mostly a hip skill
Feet going to the bar too slowly, losing all the momentum
Ending with bent arms instead of pushing to a full straight-arm support
Head dipping (looking down) which breaks the body line

quickTipsChecklist.description: Swings way out before the hips come up
Toes zip up to the bar, not slowly
The upward motion comes from the hips, not pulling
Arrives at the bar with locked, straight arms

videos.title: How to Do a Kip on Bars — Step by Step Tutorial
Kip Drills on Bars — Swing Big

name: Cast to Handstand
description: A cast is when your daughter swings her hips away from the bar while holding it — like a swing on a playground. A cast to handstand means she swings so high that she reaches a perfect vertical (handstand) position above the bar. This skill is required in every level of gymnastics from about Level 4 upward and is the foundation for almost every bars connection.
whatToLookFor: Starting from a hollow body position before the cast
Her hips shooting UP and AWAY from the bar — not just back
Achieving a true vertical position (handstand) above the bar
Legs staying together and straight throughout
Controlled return back to the bar — not just falling
commonMistakes: Only casting to 45 degrees or horizontal instead of all the way to vertical
Legs splitting apart during the cast — they should stay squeezed together
Piking at the hips (bending at the waist) on the way up
Arching the back instead of maintaining a straight body line
Rushing back to the bar without controlling the descent

quickTipsChecklist.description: Hips push upward, not just backward
Achieves true handstand above the bar
No split between the legs throughout the skill
No arch or pike — straight from shoulders to toes

videos.title: Cast to Handstand on Bars — Tutorial

name: Back Hip Circle
description: Your daughter starts sitting on top of the bar (in a front support), then tips forward and rotates backwards around the bar in a full circle, ending back where she started. Her hips stay in contact with the bar the whole way around. It looks like a slow-motion spin around the bar and is usually one of the first bars skills gymnasts learn.
whatToLookFor: A tight, hollow body — abs engaged, back not arching
Hips staying in contact with the bar throughout the spin
A controlled, smooth rotation — not a panicked, flailing spin
Ending back in the front support position with straight arms
Head staying neutral — not looking down or throwing the head back
commonMistakes: Letting the hips pull away from the bar during the rotation (this stops the spin)
Arching the back during the circle instead of staying tight and hollow
Bending the arms, which makes the whole skill fall apart
Going too fast or panicking halfway through and letting go
Landing back on top of the bar with bent arms

quickTipsChecklist.description: Belly stays in contact with the bar all the way around
Core engaged, no arching or piking
Arms stay straight and strong throughout
Controlled circle, not a panic spin

videos.title: Back Hip Circle — Beginner Bars Tutorial

name: Giant Swing
description: A giant is a full 360-degree swing around the bar while hanging with straight arms and a stretched body. Your daughter starts in a handstand above the bar and swings all the way around, passing through the lowest point (hanging below the bar) and back up to handstand again. Giants are the most important skill on bars — they generate the power for releases, pirouettes, and dismounts.
whatToLookFor: A long, stretched body shape throughout the entire swing
An obvious hollow position as she comes through the bottom of the swing
The handstand at the top looking solid and vertical
Arms staying straight — any bent arms receive an automatic deduction
No piking at the hips — her body should look like one long line
commonMistakes: A 'banana' back (arch) instead of a hollow body position
Bending the arms at any point, especially at the top going into handstand
Piking at the hips in the bottom of the swing
Shoulders creeping up to the ears instead of staying down and strong
Losing the body line coming up on the back side of the swing

quickTipsChecklist.description: From fingertips to toes she looks tall and tight
Core engaged as she swings through the lowest point
Elbows locked throughout the entire giant
Reaches a true handstand at the top

videos.title: Giant Swing on Bars — Gymnastics Tutorial
